Track: Getting Started with R

R
BA
A beginner learning path for students with no prior programming experience who want to use R for data analysis — from installation through data objects, project setup, and first workflows.
Author

Claudius Gräbner-Radkowitsch

Published

June 16, 2026

Modified

June 16, 2026

1 Who This Track Is For

This track is for students who have never used R before and want to reach the point where they can load, inspect, and work with data confidently. No prior programming experience is assumed — a working computer and a willingness to experiment are all you need.

By the end of this track you will be able to:

  • Install R, RStudio, and packages on your own machine
  • Navigate the RStudio interface and write basic R code
  • Understand R’s core object types: functions, vectors, factors, and data frames
  • Organise your work in a reproducible R project with a sensible folder structure

This track ends where data work begins. After completing it, continue with the Data Workflow in R track to load, clean, and visualise real data.

2 Core Sequence

2.1 1. Installation

Getting Started with R: Installing Everything You Need

Install R and RStudio, orient yourself in the RStudio interface, and run your first commands in the console. Start here if you have never opened R before.

2.2 2. Packages

Installing R Packages

Understand what packages are, install them from CRAN, and load them into a session. Covers the difference between install.packages() and library() and how to find packages you need.

2.3 3. First Steps

First Steps in R

Write your first R code: arithmetic, variable assignment, and basic operations. Develops the mental model of R as an interactive calculator before introducing more structured programming.

2.4 4. Functions

Object Types in R I: Functions

Understand how functions work in R — arguments, defaults, return values, and how to read help documentation. Functions are the primary way of doing anything in R; this page makes them legible.

2.5 5. Vectors

Object Types in R II: Vectors

The fundamental data structure in R: atomic vectors, their types, how to create and index them, and how vectorised operations work. Understanding vectors makes everything else in R predictable.

2.6 6. Factors and Data Frames

Object Types in R III: Factors and Data Frames

Factors encode categorical variables; data frames hold rectangular datasets. This page covers both — how to create them, access their contents, and why factors behave differently from character vectors.

2.7 7. Project Setup

Setting Up an R Project

Create an RStudio project, organise files into a sensible folder structure, and use here::here() for portable file paths. Good project hygiene from the start makes your work reproducible and shareable.

3 All R Programming Resources

Title Description
Data Preparation with the Tidyverse How to reshape data between wide and long format with tidyr, manipulate variables and filter rows with dplyr, merge data sets, and chain steps together using pipes.
Exercise: Predicting Firm Exit with Logistic Regression Practice logistic regression end-to-end: fit a model for binary firm exit, extend it with additional predictors, interpret odds ratios, compute scenario-based predicted probabilities, and communicate findings with inline R code.
Experiments in R How to analyze experimental data in R: t-tests for simple comparisons, one-way and two-way ANOVA for multi-group designs, effect sizes, post-hoc tests, and power analysis.
First Steps in R Learn the basic syntax of R: how to issue commands, use objects, assignments, and functions, and how to install and use R packages.
Getting Started with Quarto An introduction to Quarto — the modern successor to R Markdown — covering how to create documents that mix text and code, control output rendering, and produce HTML and PDF reports.
Importing and Exporting Data in R How to read CSV files into R using data.table::fread(), handle common format variations (separators, decimal signs, leading zeros), and save data to CSV, RDS, RData, and Stata formats.
Getting Started with R: Installing Everything You Need Step-by-step guide to installing R, RStudio, Git, Quarto, and registering for GitHub and Netlify so you have a complete working environment for data science.
Installing R Packages How to install the R packages needed for data science work, verify your installation using the DataScienceExercises package, and set up LaTeX for PDF output.
Linear Regression in R Practical implementation of simple and multiple linear regression in R: fitting models with lm(), interpreting coefficients, computing R², understanding omitted variable bias, applying log and quadratic transformations, and reading diagnostic plots.
Logarithms: A Gentle Introduction A practical introduction to logarithms for data science: what they are, the natural logarithm, the relationship with exponential functions, and when and why to use log transformations in statistical analysis.
Logistic Regression for Binary Outcomes in R Understand why OLS fails for binary outcomes, fit logistic regression with glm(), and translate log-odds into predicted probabilities and average marginal effects.
Categorical Variables and Interaction Terms in Regression Extend linear regression to handle categorical predictors with multiple levels and interaction terms, using a real earnings dataset to explore how the age–earnings profile differs by gender.
Panel Data and Fixed Effects in R Understand the logic of panel data, learn to distinguish within- from between-unit variation, estimate fixed effects models with fixest, and connect the FE identification argument to causal reasoning.
Publication-Ready Figures with ggplot2 Move beyond exploratory plots to publication-ready figures: choose the right chart type, apply the 7-item checklist, and use patchwork, ggrepel, gghighlight, ggtext, and scales to produce submission-quality output.
Object Types in R I: Functions An introduction to functions as R objects: how to call them in prefix and infix form, how to work with arguments, and how to define and document your own functions.
Object Types in R II: Vectors A thorough introduction to atomic vectors and lists in R: types (logical, integer, double, character), coercion, logical operations, vectorization, indexing, and useful functions.
Object Types in R III: Factors and Data Frames An introduction to factors and data frames in R: how they are built upon base types, how to create and manipulate them, and the tibble as a modern alternative.
Regression Residuals and Model Diagnostics Understand the distinction between residuals and error terms, learn the key assumptions of linear regression, and apply visual and quantitative diagnostic tests to evaluate model quality.
Setting Up an R Project How to create a well-structured R project directory, understand working directories and file paths, and use the here package to write portable, reproducible code.
Using AI Tools for R Coding How to use AI assistants deliberately and critically in R programming — understanding what AI can and cannot do, three modes of effective use, the failure modes to watch for, and the one habit that makes AI assistance durable.
Visualization with ggplot2 How to build publication-quality plots in R using ggplot2: the layer-by-layer workflow, mapping aesthetics, adding geoms, adjusting scales, themes, and saving plots.
No matching items

# Who This Track Is For

This track is for students who have never used R before and want to reach the point where they can load, inspect, and work with data confidently. No prior programming experience is assumed — a working computer and a willingness to experiment are all you need.

By the end of this track you will be able to:

- Install R, RStudio, and packages on your own machine
- Navigate the RStudio interface and write basic R code
- Understand R's core object types: functions, vectors, factors, and data frames
- Organise your work in a reproducible R project with a sensible folder structure

This track ends where data work begins. After completing it, continue with the [Data Workflow in R](data-workflow-in-r.qmd) track to load, clean, and visualise real data.

---

# Core Sequence

## 1. Installation

[Getting Started with R: Installing Everything You Need](../resources/r-programming/installing-r-and-rstudio.qmd)

Install R and RStudio, orient yourself in the RStudio interface, and run your first commands in the console. Start here if you have never opened R before.

## 2. Packages

[Installing R Packages](../resources/r-programming/installing-r-packages.qmd)

Understand what packages are, install them from CRAN, and load them into a session. Covers the difference between `install.packages()` and `library()` and how to find packages you need.

## 3. First Steps

[First Steps in R](../resources/r-programming/first-steps-in-r.qmd)

Write your first R code: arithmetic, variable assignment, and basic operations. Develops the mental model of R as an interactive calculator before introducing more structured programming.

## 4. Functions

[Object Types in R I: Functions](../resources/r-programming/r-object-types-i-functions.qmd)

Understand how functions work in R — arguments, defaults, return values, and how to read help documentation. Functions are the primary way of doing anything in R; this page makes them legible.

## 5. Vectors

[Object Types in R II: Vectors](../resources/r-programming/r-object-types-ii-vectors.qmd)

The fundamental data structure in R: atomic vectors, their types, how to create and index them, and how vectorised operations work. Understanding vectors makes everything else in R predictable.

## 6. Factors and Data Frames

[Object Types in R III: Factors and Data Frames](../resources/r-programming/r-object-types-iii-factors-dataframes.qmd)

Factors encode categorical variables; data frames hold rectangular datasets. This page covers both — how to create them, access their contents, and why factors behave differently from character vectors.

## 7. Project Setup

[Setting Up an R Project](../resources/r-programming/setting-up-an-r-project.qmd)

Create an RStudio project, organise files into a sensible folder structure, and use `here::here()` for portable file paths. Good project hygiene from the start makes your work reproducible and shareable.

---

# All R Programming Resources
